medications.indication_rationale: For the secondary cellulitis; oral now, escalate to IV if systemically unwell/spreading.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 500 mg orally four times daily for ~7 days (as per source note; per prescriber)

medications.indication_rationale: Elevate the leg above heart level (correct for a venous ulcer), manage heavy exudate with an absorbent dressing, and provide analgesia.

medications.drug_name: tight graduated compression during the acute infection / before ABPI
medications.indication_rationale: Do NOT resume tight compression during acute infection/severe pain or before arterial disease is excluded (ABPI); reinstate once the acute cellulitis settles.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Keep the leg elevated above heart level when sitting, keep the dressing dry, and do NOT reapply tight compression stockings until the acute infection and severe pain settle and a clinician has checked the circulation.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Reduces venous congestion/oedema safely while avoiding harm during acute infection.

patient_education_points.point: Your leg-vein problem caused this ankle ulcer, and it now has a skin infection (cellulitis). The antibiotics treat the infection, and keeping the leg raised reduces the fluid pressure so it can heal.

Don't put your tight compression stockings back on yet — not during the infection/severe pain, and not until a clinician checks your circulation. A wound clinic will restart compression once it settles.
If the redness rapidly spreads up your calf, the whole leg becomes swollen and severely painful, or you develop a high fever/shivering — go to the emergency department.

follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Cellulitis response, wound/exudate, ABPI, and re-initiation of compression once settled.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Ascending calf redness, leg-wide swelling, or high fever/rigors — emergency care.

must_include_items: Recognise a venous ulcer with secondary cellulitis and treat with antibiotics + wound care
Advise LEG ELEVATION (correct for venous) and do NOT resume tight compression during acute infection / before ABPI
Urgent community wound-clinic referral and ABPI before compression
acceptable_alternatives: Alternative antibiotic per allergy/sensitivities; IV if systemically unwell
errors_of_commission: Advising tight compression during acute infection or before ABPI
Confusing it with an arterial ulcer (advising dependency)
Managing entirely remotely without wound-clinic referral
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: Pass requires antibiotics + ELEVATION + wound-clinic referral with compression deferred until infection settles/ABPI done, and a sepsis safety-net. Advising compression now, or dependency (arterial advice), are key failures.
